vocabulary.word: talk show / quiz show / game show

phrasal_verbs.meaning: try to find information in a book or list, etc
pretend that something is true; see, hear or understand sb or sth with difficulty
invent an explanation, excuse, etc; create a story, poem, etc
add information in the spaces on a document
turn and look at the pages of a magazine, etc quickly
recognise that sth is not true and not be tricked by it
be easy to see because of being different
give things to people in a group
turn a page so that the other side is towards you

question: ask/answer a question
question sth/sb
in question
question mark
comment: make a comment (to sb) about sth

A.title: Topic vocabulary in contrast
A.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the words in the box.

questions.sentence: The celebrity ...... any knowledge of the leaked photos when questioned by reporters.
The minister ...... to answer questions about the budget during the live interview.
The veteran actor rarely ...... requests to appear on chat shows.
The director ...... to give an exclusive interview to promote the new film.
Did you catch the ...... on the front page this morning?
Each chapter of the report had a clear ...... so it was easy to navigate.
The magazine ran a long ...... this month on climate change and young activists.
There was a fascinating ...... in the weekend supplement about deep-sea creatures.
The actor said she trusted social media more than the ...... when it came to sharing news.
In today's world, the ...... include everything from online blogs to 24-hour news channels.
We are interrupting this programme to bring you an urgent .......
The traffic ...... at the top of the hour warned of delays on the motorway.

B.title: Circle the correct word
B.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

questions.sentence: I downloaded a new **programme / program** to manage my files on the laptop.
He gets all his news from **tabloid / broadsheet** websites that focus on celebrity gossip.
She doesn't go out to cover stories; as a **journalist / columnist** for a national paper, she only writes opinion pieces.
There's a new **talk / quiz / game** show where teams race through a theme park collecting items.
On some TV channels, an **announcer / a commentator** introduces programmes and tells viewers what is coming up next.
We stayed up to watch a live **broadcast / channel** of the final of the tennis championship.

C.title: Phrasal verbs (gap fill)
C.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

questions.sentence: Please do not ...... over the page until the examiner says you can begin.
You will need to ...... in a form to apply for the media pass.
Could everyone ...... forward their ideas during the editorial meeting?
There was a promoter outside the cinema ...... out free tickets for tomorrow's preview.
He ...... out that he had worked as a foreign correspondent, but he had never left the country.
Could you ...... up the broadcast times for that documentary?
Her reporting ...... out for its clarity and depth.

D.title: Phrasal verbs (transformation)
D.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

questions.sentence:
The journalist's autobiography was published last week. **out**
The journalist's autobiography ...... last week.

That headline wasn't accurate, was it? **up**
You ......, didn't you?

The documentary starts at nine o'clock. **on**
The documentary ...... at nine o'clock.

The article didn't really explore the causes of the crisis. **into**
The article didn't really ...... the causes of the crisis.

I had a quick look at the magazine in the waiting room. **through**
I ...... in the waiting room.

I don't think we should mention the scandal now. **up**
I don't think we should ...... now.

Her false claims don't fool me! **through**
I can ...... her false claims!
Reporters often struggle to ...... the difference between reliable and unreliable sources.
The press conference will ...... place at midday.
In my ......, all news outlets should aim to be unbiased.
The new editor has ...... control of the sports section.
The witness ...... a description of the suspect to the police.
This documentary has ...... a huge influence on public awareness.
She spends hours every day ...... the Internet for the latest news.
It was reported ...... the news that a major agreement had been signed.
The article ...... question raises some serious concerns.
Watch ...... for misleading statistics in online articles.

F.title: Error correction (Word patterns)
F.instructions: Each of the words in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

questions.sentence: She has been praised by her readers **for** a truly original voice.
Many people confuse a newsreader **by** a journalist.
Do you believe **about** the power of the press?
There's no point **of** applying to that station. They're not recruiting right now.
According **from** the survey, more people now get their news online than from newspapers.
The statistics in this report don't correspond **about** what we found in our own research.

G.title: Cloze (Word patterns)
G.instructions: Water has damaged part of this text. Read it and decide what you think each of the original words was. Write the words in the blank spaces.

questions.sentence: The producer sent an urgent message ...... every reporter on the news desk, announcing a live broadcast at noon.
I had already heard ...... the breaking story and had heard ...... a colleague ...... there had been a major development.
Whatever it was, it was likely ...... be significant.
Before the broadcast, the director told us ...... share any details with anyone outside the building.
She said she had only been informed ...... the full story that morning.
We were all so stunned ...... the news that we sat in complete silence.
The presenter made a comment ...... the editor that it would be the biggest story of the year.
The editor heard this and managed to persuade us ...... rush out without checking all the facts first.

H.title: Word formation (sentences)
H.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

questions.sentence: The celebrity arrived at the premiere completely ...... (ANNOUNCE), surprising all the journalists.
Social media has transformed the way ...... (COMMUNICATE) works in the modern world.
The editor asked her to write an ...... (EDIT) about the rise of online news platforms.
You certainly need a sense of ...... (HUMOUR) to survive as a late-night chat show host.
There's an ...... (WRITE) rule in broadcasting that you never interrupt the headline news.
By the time she finished ...... (SECOND) school, she had already written for the local paper.
After years in television, he decided to return to print ...... (JOURNAL).
You won't find much ...... (INFORM) about local events in a national newspaper.

I.title: Word formation (text)
I.instructions: Complete the text by changing the form of the word in capitals.

questions.sentence: I enjoy watching (1) ...... (DISCUSS) panels on TV, and I'm passionate about current affairs, so you'd think I'd find watching (2) ...... (POLITICS) debate in public fascinating.
But I often don't. Too frequently, (3) ...... (JOURNAL) ask them the most (4) ...... (RIDICULE) questions,
and when they do pose an interesting one, I watch in (5) ...... (BELIEVE) as some of the most (6) ...... (POWER) figures in the country give completely (7) ...... (CONVINCE) answers.
It is as though they don't care whether their reply sounds (8) ...... (BELIEVE) or not.
Many are poor (9) ...... (COMMUNICATE) and appear even more (10) ...... (INFORM) about key issues than the average viewer.
I don't necessarily expect them to be particularly (11) ...... (HUMOUR) – politics is a serious business – but surely they could say something memorable once in a while.
